main_summary.summary: Julia Matilda Burns, interviewed in Tchula, Mississippi on March 13, 2013 by John Dittmer with videographer John Bishop, describes her trajectory from a Farm Security Administration homestead in Humphreys County to a career as a Holmes County teacher, school board member, and Civil Rights Movement collaborator. Born in Greenwood and raised on 123 FSA-purchased acres after her father moved the family from town to country in the early 1940s, Burns graduated from Humphreys County Training School in 1954 and earned an English degree at Mississippi Valley State under founding president James Herbert White. She student-taught in Belzoni, briefly moved to Chicago after marriage, and returned to Tchula in 1962, where principal S.B. Marshall hired her at Marshall High School. Burns describes operating behind the scenes during the SNCC and MFDP organizing years, hosting Freedom Summer workers including Henry and Sue Lorenzi, Abe Osheroff (who helped build the Mileston Community Center), and SNCC organizer John Ball from Greenwood. Her son was among the first-grade cohort that desegregated Tchula Consolidated School in 1965, after which the white population departed for Cruger-Tchula Academy. Elected to the Holmes County School Board around 1971, she also served as secretary of the Mileston Cooperative, coordinating Dr. Demitri Shimkin's University of Illinois Holmes County Health Research Program.
key_themes: Mileston Cooperative and SNCC support work
Black educator activism on school boards
main_summary.historical_significance:
Burns's testimony documents the intersection of Black landownership through FSA homesteading, Black educator activism during the segregation-to-integration transition, and academic-community partnerships between the University of Illinois and Holmes County's grassroots organizing infrastructure. Her account of operating as a teacher behind the scenes, hosting Freedom Summer workers at her home, feeding them lemonade and tea cakes, while keeping a public low profile to preserve her teaching position, preserves a specific organizational pattern that civil rights scholarship often overlooks.

Her firsthand account of her son's participation in the 1965 first-grade desegregation of Tchula Consolidated School, the school's serial burnings, and the white community's wholesale exit to Cruger-Tchula Academy provides primary-source detail on Holmes County's particular pattern of segregation academy formation. Her recollection of serving on the Holmes County School Board alongside Dr. Arenia C. Mallory of Saints Industrial School, and her role as secretary linking the Mileston Cooperative to Dr. Demitri Shimkin's Holmes County Health Research Program, document an underexamined network connecting cooperative organizing, public health research, and the Robert F. Kennedy economic development initiatives that followed his 1967 Mississippi Delta poverty tour.

chapters.title: A Hundred Twenty-Three Acres in Humphreys County
chapters.topic: Born in Greenwood, Raised on FSA Land
chapters.summary: Interviewed by John Dittmer in Tchula, Mississippi, in March 2013, Julia Matilda Burns recounts being born in Greenwood to Margaret and Trevenu Griffin, and her father moving the family to Humphreys County in the early 1940s to buy roughly 123 acres of his own through the Farm Security Administration, a place with cotton, corn, cane, and livestock that she calls a stepping stone to progress.

chapters.title: Grew Up Overnight
chapters.topic: A Mother's Death and an Early Start in School
chapters.summary: Burns describes her brother leaving for New York to work for an auto maker and send money home for her education, her mother passing when Burns was thirteen so that she became head of the household as a girl, part farmer and part housekeeper, and how she had started school at four and a half partly to keep her brother out of fights.

chapters.title: Two Teachers, Grades One Through Eight
chapters.topic: A One-Room School and Consolidation
chapters.summary: She remembers a school heated by a pot-bellied stove where two teachers taught all eight grades in one open room, learning from the classes ahead, then the consolidation that created Womack and Hodge, later Womack High School, before students went on to Humphreys County Training School in Louise, Mississippi.

chapters.title: News Is News
chapters.topic: Mississippi Valley and the Brown Decision
chapters.summary: Burns recounts finishing high school in 1954 and going on to Mississippi Valley State, and how the Brown v. Board of Education decision that same year did not register with her at the time, with nothing changing in Mississippi schools, no new buildings, materials, or textbooks brought in to make segregation look equal.

chapters.title: The Picture in Jet
chapters.topic: Remembering Emmett Till
chapters.summary: Burns recalls reading about the Emmett Till case in 1955 in Jet magazine, which her parents had delivered to the home, the horrifying picture of the body, her father warning her brother not to whistle at or bother white girls, the Belzoni boys who openly had white girlfriends, and the trial that set the killers free.

chapters.title: Baptist, Methodist, and the Sanctified Church
chapters.topic: A Family of Faith
chapters.summary: Burns describes growing up between a Baptist and a Methodist church with a sanctified church further down the road, attending the nearer Baptist church every Sunday though Methodist by faith, getting rides from friends to the sanctified church, and remaining Methodist by her own choice.

chapters.title: She Saw What I Could Not See in Myself
chapters.topic: A Tenth-Grade English Teacher
chapters.summary: Burns credits her tenth-grade English teacher as her major influence, a very smart woman who urged her to go to college and major in English, in an era when teachers visited families and took an interest in children's futures, and recounts telling her father, who agreed to help her become an English teacher.

chapters.title: I Rode the Bus to College
chapters.topic: Reading, Tennyson, and the Commute
chapters.summary: Burns recounts being the only sibling to go to college at the time, riding the bus three miles from home because there was no money to live on campus, having access to library books she could take home, and her love of Alfred Lord Tennyson and poetry in tune with nature, alongside her reaction to reading Eldridge Cleaver's Soul on Ice.

chapters.title: Teachers for the Delta
chapters.topic: Mississippi Valley State's Early Years
chapters.summary: Burns describes Mississippi Valley State, built around 1950 with only a few buildings and everything taught in one classroom building, a small student body and an education-focused curriculum, founded by Dr. James Herbert White out of Tennessee with the goal of developing teachers for the Delta, where she graduated in 1959.

chapters.part: Marriage and the Cold of Chicago
chapters.title: Student Teaching and a Marriage
chapters.topic: First Job, Marriage, and Moving North
chapters.summary: Burns recounts doing her student teaching in Belzoni where she grew up, naming productive former students like Clara Reed, getting her first teaching job and marrying in her second year, then moving with her husband to Chicago, where he worked as a salesman at Wieboldt's and she found the city and the cold not for her.

chapters.part: Marriage and the Cold of Chicago
chapters.title: One Bale of Cotton
chapters.topic: Back to the Farm and Belzoni's Reputation
chapters.summary: Burns describes returning from Chicago so her husband could farm his family's Farm Security Administration land, getting loans through the FSA, living off the land and a community grist mill, and Belzoni's reputation as a town so tough that people picked up their goods and hurried out, a place civil rights workers later called dangerous.

chapters.title: You'll Lose Your Job
chapters.topic: A Teacher Behind the Scenes
chapters.summary: Burns recounts coming back to teach at Marshall High School under principal S.B. Marshall, her husband farming while working with SNCC and later the Child Development Group of Mississippi, and a man named John Ball out of Greenwood warning her at the school that she would lose her job if she did not leave the freedom workers alone.

chapters.title: Lemonade and Tea Cakes
chapters.topic: Supporting the Freedom Workers Quietly
chapters.summary: Burns describes freedom workers parking behind her house so no one would see, her cooking refreshments for them as they passed, being active behind the scenes while keeping a low key for fear of losing her job, and the arrival of Abe Osheroff to build the Mileston Community Center where the community began to meet.

chapters.title: You Understand Fear
chapters.topic: Henry Lorenzi and the Voter Workshops
chapters.summary: Burns recalls the work sessions preparing people to register and vote, the organizers Henry and Sue Lorenzi, Henry telling her that people thought she understood fear, and her husband and others, including Reverend Whitaker and several women teachers, teaching registration classes that helped launch the Child Development Group's Head Start program.

chapters.title: They Burned the School Down
chapters.topic: Integrating Tchula Consolidated
chapters.summary: Burns recounts the 1965 federal court order to desegregate Holmes County schools, her young son being one of about sixteen Black first graders sent to integrate the white Tchula Consolidated School, whites burning down part of the school to scare them off, and the children and parents refusing to leave.

chapters.title: All the Whites Left
chapters.topic: Resegregation and Cruger-Tchula Academy
chapters.summary: Burns describes her son's classmate Christopher Epps, who later directed corrections, the white students and teachers all leaving once Black children arrived so the school turned entirely Black, and the whites founding the segregated Cruger-Tchula Academy rather than attend integrated schools.

chapters.title: Going to School for the Right Reason
chapters.topic: Returning to Teach and College Aspirations
chapters.summary: Burns recounts coming back to teach in 1967 after two years out, her students going on to historically Black colleges like Mississippi Valley State and Jackson State, the later arrival of white-college recruiters and Ole Miss admission, and her concern that some students now chase grant money or earn online degrees rather than going to school for a real purpose.

chapters.part: A Seat on the School Board
chapters.title: He Died Before the Election
chapters.topic: Running for the School Board
chapters.summary: Burns explains running for the school board because as a parent and teacher she knew education's problems and wanted to fix them, campaigning through churches and communities rather than door to door, winning, and the white incumbent Martin Smith dying the night or two before the election after a Black person opposed him for the first time.

chapters.part: A Seat on the School Board
chapters.title: She Saw Things the Way White Folks Saw Things
chapters.topic: Dr. Arenia C. Mallory and Board Issues
chapters.summary: Burns recalls serving as one of two Black members on a five-person board with three whites, alongside Dr. Arenia C. Mallory of the Saints Industrial School, who warned Burns against asking for too much for Black folks while Burns carried teachers' and parents' demands to the board, dealing with staffing, funding, buildings, and teacher shortages.

chapters.part: A Seat on the School Board
chapters.title: The Academies Phase Out
chapters.topic: White Flight, Durant, and a Black Superintendent
chapters.summary: Burns describes the white academies phasing out so white teachers had nowhere to go, a separate white school district set up in Durant for those who could not afford the academies until it too went majority Black, hiring younger white teachers who worked out well, and the first Black superintendent, William Dean, arriving in the late 1980s.

chapters.title: A Child of Six With Hypertension
chapters.topic: The Shimkin Study and Community Health
chapters.summary: Burns recounts her role in the research behind Dr. Demitri Shimkin's book The Extended Family in Black Societies, growing out of the Mileston Cooperative and the Holmes County Health Research Program, going house to house and finding widespread hypertension even in a six-year-old child, while serving as secretary and go-between with the University of Illinois.

chapters.title: We Didn't Know We Were Poor
chapters.topic: School Lunches, a Clinic, and Migration
chapters.summary: Burns describes recommendations from the study, the Robert F. Kennedy economic development effort, starting breakfast and lunch programs for schoolchildren, developing a health clinic, studying population shift as plantation families moved into city apartments and migrated to Chicago, Peoria, and Wisconsin, and how civil rights workers showed people they were poor.

chapters.title: I Teach French
chapters.topic: Thirty-Five Years and a Job She Loves
chapters.summary: Burns closes on her career after leaving the school board in 1976, teaching English and literature at Mississippi Valley and then in the Holmes County public schools, going back to school at Mississippi Valley to learn French after the state mandated a foreign language, and at retirement teaching French three hours a day part time in a job she loves.

loc_video.caption: Julia Matilda Burns oral history interview conducted by John Dittmer in Tchula, Mississippi, 2013 March 13
